course_description:
Biomedical Science Research Techniques is a preparative course for Biomedical Science Honours students and forms 2 units of the 16-unit program. An in-depth understanding of biomedical research requires knowledge of the experimental techniques used to generate scientific data. Students will learn foundational skills in biostatistics, hypothesis testing, experimental design, and research integrity. A focus on selected research techniques will round out student’s preparation and include in depth background knowledge of the chosen techniques, hands-on demonstrations of critical technologies and analysis of captured datasets. Students will be assessed on their understanding of a range of research techniques and biostatistics.

It has been estimated that around 70% of scientists have tried and failed to replicate published experiments. What is behind this replication crisis? Can an honest scientist publish disreputable or non-reproducible work? What is p-hacking? A knowledge of best practice experimental design and data analysis can help you stay out of these traps and lay the foundations for a successful and impactful career in research. Furthermore, what are the best techniques to use to address your scientific questions? How do I determine if two proteins interact? How can I see what cells express a gene or protein of interest? How do I survey the diverse cell types and gene profiles expressed in normal versus diseased tissues? How can I start to determine the mechanisms underlying a physiological response? To answer these questions and more, you will need to arm yourself with knowledge of both standard and cutting-edge techniques in the biomedical sciences space.

In BIOM6080 you will learn about best practices in experimental design and data analysis through case studies and lectures. You will get hands on experience with several exemplars of biomedical science techniques, learning not only how the techniques work, but their limitations and specific data analysis. The goal of the course is to promote an exploration of experimental design and techniques that can lay the foundations for you to apply this knowledge to your current honours research project and beyond. While some of the techniques you will explore in BIOM6080 may not be directly applicable to your current honours project, they are meant to be exemplars for the level of understanding you will need to acquire for any technical method you choose to apply in your research. You will come away from BIOM6080 with scientific skills you can transfer across your subsequent research endeavours and underpin your development as an impactful biomedical research scientist.

restrictions: Restricted to students enrolled in BBiomedSc(Hons); or BSc(Hons) or BAdvSc(Hons) majoring in Biomedical Science or Cell Biology only.

course_contacts.name: Student and Academic Administration Team
course_contacts.notes: The SBMS Student and Academic Administration Team is located on Level 1 of the Sir William Macgregor Building (64-130).

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims: The course will develop skills that are valued by employers across a range of professions and industries that are possible career destinations for Biomedical Science graduates. These skills include critical analysis, scientific creativity, problem solving, experimental design and testing, technical expertise, and effective communication. This course lays the foundation for students wishing to progress to Higher Degree by Research (HDR) studies and contributes to generic skill development and job readiness for a broad range of other career options. Moreover, the course aims to bolster the preparedness of students commencing genuine research experiences in their honours year to maximise their research outputs and achievements.

learning_outcomes.description: Evaluate and analyse the theory behind different biomedical techniques.
Develop skills in qualitative and quantitative data analysis and presentation.
Evaluate and incorporate biostatistics into experimental design and data analysis.
Recognise and apply good laboratory practices consistent with high standards of research integrity.
Work and learn collaboratively, leveraging the diverse abilities, backgrounds, and perspectives within the group.

assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You must submit your Assessment task, with the electronic coversheet available on the School's website (under Undergraduate - Assessment), to Turnitin by the submission deadline. You may submit drafts to Turnitin up to the due date, but you will only be able to submit once on or after the due date. You should also keep an electronic copy of every piece of assessment you submit.
If you are experiencing technical difficulties uploading your submission, please email a copy of your assessment to sbms@enquire.uq.edu.au so this can be logged on your behalf before the due date.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
This assessment has a maximum extension of 7 days to prevent overlap with subsequent assessments.
A request for an extension must be submitted through my.UQ as soon as it becomes evident that an extension is needed, but no later than one calendar day after the assessment task submission due date and time.
Requests for extension received more than one calendar day after the assessment task submission due date and time will not be accepted for consideration unless you provide evidence of exceptional circumstances preventing you from submitting a request prior to the deadline, e.g., due to hospitalisation.
If you have been granted an extension, then the assessment specified penalty listed under Late Submission will be applied to submissions made after the due date of the approved extension
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Week 1 Week one includes an initial course introduction and cohort building workshop on the Monday, followed by workshops and lectures around the topics of the scientific method and hypothesis testing on Thursday. Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L05

Week 2 Week 2 consists of workshops and lectures on experimental design, biostatistics, and research ethics. These workshops run half a day on Monday and all day Thursday. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 3 Week 3 consists of workshops and lectures on bioinformatics. These workshops run half a day on Monday and all day Thursday. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 4 In week 4 students will attend a module on Microscopy. Workshops focused on microscopy run half a day on Monday and all day Thursday. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Week 5 In week 5 students will attend one of three workshops run concurrently. Details of the assignment will be made available on Blackboard. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
